skill.name: cc-skill-coding-standards
skill.description: Coding standards and review guidance for TypeScript, JavaScript, React, Node.js, API design, testing, and maintainable project structure.
skill.summary: Apply consistent JavaScript, TypeScript, React, and Node.js coding standards during implementation and review.
skill.author: affaan-m

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are documentation examples, Markdown code fences, or ordinary prose in SKILL.md. No executable scripts, command execution behavior, credential access, data exfiltration, or prompt injection attempts were found. The skill is low risk because the detected patterns are false positives in a community documentation skill.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections point to Markdown code fences and TypeScript examples. They are not instructions to execute shell commands and no command invocation behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are fenced documentation examples in SKILL.md. They contain code samples for style guidance, not executable skill logic or shell command construction.

low_findings.title: Fetch API Examples Are Documentation Only
low_findings.description: The network detections are sample fetch calls inside an error handling section. They demonstrate application code style and do not send data to a fixed external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both fetch calls are inside TypeScript examples that use a generic url parameter. No real host, credential, or exfiltration payload is present.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Example Is a React Lazy Loading Pattern
low_findings.description: The dynamic import detection is a React lazy loading example for a local component path. It is not runtime plugin loading or remote code execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The import expression references a local component path in a fenced React example. No user input, remote URL, or executable skill script is involved.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Prose False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm detections align with ordinary words such as description or descriptive headings. No cryptographic implementation or algorithm selection is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are front matter, headings, or validation examples. They do not contain MD5, DES, SHA1 usage, or any cryptographic operation.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are Benign Text
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance detections refer to prose, REST endpoint examples, or coding guidance. No host enumeration, environment probing, or system discovery command appears.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation content and sample API routes. No commands or APIs collect local system information.

content.user_title: Apply Coding Standards Across JavaScript Projects
content.value_statement: Teams need consistent code quality guidance across TypeScript, JavaScript, React, and Node.js projects. This skill gives practical standards for naming, structure, validation, testing, and maintainability.
actual_capabilities: Guides naming, readability, immutability, and type safety choices for TypeScript and JavaScript code.
Provides React patterns for typed components, custom hooks, state updates, and conditional rendering.
Explains REST API conventions, response formats, input validation, and file organization patterns.
Defines testing guidance for clear test names and arrange, act, assert structure.
Highlights common code smells such as long functions, deep nesting, and magic numbers.
limitations: It is a guidance skill and does not run automated linting or formatting tools.
It focuses on TypeScript, JavaScript, React, and Node.js rather than every programming language.
It provides general standards and may need adaptation for an existing team style guide.
It does not replace security review, performance profiling, or framework-specific documentation.

use_cases.title: Standardize Pull Request Reviews
use_cases.description: Use the skill to check naming, structure, tests, and maintainability during code review.

use_cases.title: Guide Feature Implementation
use_cases.description: Use the standards while building React, API, and TypeScript features in a consistent style.

use_cases.title: Onboard Contributors
use_cases.description: Give new contributors a practical checklist for project structure, testing, and code smell avoidance.

prompt_templates.title: Review Basic Code Quality
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small change
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this TypeScript or JavaScript change using the coding standards. Focus on naming, readability, immutability, and simple fixes first.

prompt_templates.title: Improve a React Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the React standards to this component. Check props typing, state updates, conditional rendering, custom hook opportunities, and component clarity.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an API Handler
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this API handler against the standards. Review REST conventions, response shape, input validation, error handling, and test coverage gaps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Project Refactor Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this project structure and propose a focused refactor plan. Prioritize code smells, file organization, reusable utilities, and low-risk changes.

output_examples.input: Review a React button component with untyped props and direct state updates.
output: The component should define an explicit props interface.
State updates should use a functional update when based on the previous value.
The button variant handling should use clear allowed values.

output_examples.input: Check an API route that returns inconsistent success and error responses.
output: Use one response shape for success, error, and metadata.
Validate request input before business logic runs.
Return clear status codes with concise user-facing error messages.

output_examples.input: Find maintainability issues in a large utility function.
output: Split validation, transformation, and persistence into named functions.
Replace unexplained numeric values with constants.
Use early returns to reduce nested conditions.

best_practices: Apply the standards before large refactors so recommendations stay focused and practical.
Ask for findings grouped by readability, correctness, testing, and structure.
Adapt recommendations to the project style when local conventions already exist.
anti_patterns: Do not treat the guidance as a substitute for running tests, type checks, or linters.
Do not apply every rule mechanically when project constraints justify a documented exception.
Do not mix unrelated refactors with feature work only to satisfy style preferences.

faq.question: What languages does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on TypeScript, JavaScript, React, and Node.js development patterns.

faq.question: Can it replace a linter?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance, while linters and formatters enforce rules automatically.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: It includes validation and error handling guidance, but it is not a full security audit skill.

faq.question: Is it useful for code reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives reviewers a consistent checklist for readability, tests, APIs, and code smells.

faq.question: Can teams customize the standards?
faq.answer: Yes. Teams should adapt the recommendations to existing project conventions and tooling.

faq.question: Does it generate production code?
faq.answer: It guides code changes and reviews, but final implementation still depends on the project context.
